title: Finding your place in the story of Jesus. Part 2
scripture_text: ‘’Now Jesus himself was about thirty years old when he began his ministry. He was the son, so it was thought, of Joseph, the son of Heli, the son of Matthat, the son of Levi, the son of Melki, the son of Jannai, the son of Joseph, the son of Mattathias, the son of Amos, the son of Nahum, the son of Esli, the son of Naggai, the son of Maath, the son of Mattathias, the son of Semein, the son of Josek, the son of Joda, the son of Joanan, the son of Rhesa, the son of Zerubbabel, the son of Shealtiel, the son of Neri the son of Melki, the son of Addi, the son of Cosam, the son of Elmadam, the son of Er, the son of Joshua, the son of Eliezer, the son of Jorim, the son of Matthat, he son of Levi, the son of Simeon the son of Judah, the son of Joseph, the son of Jonam, the son of Eliakim, the son of Melea, the son of Menna, the son of Mattatha, the son of Nathan, the son of David, the son of Jesse, the son of Obed, the son of Boaz, the son of Salmon, the son of Nahshon, the son of Amminadab, the son of Ram the son of Hezron, the son of Perez, the son of Judah, the son of Jacob, the son of Isaac, the son of Abraham, the son of Terah, the son of Nahor, the son of Serug, the son of Reu, the son of Peleg, the son of Eber, the son of Shelah, the son of Cainan, the son of Arphaxad, the son of Shem, the son of Noah, the son of Lamech, the son of Methuselah, the son of Enoch, the son of Jared, the son of Mahalalel, the son of Kenan, the son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God.’’
devotional:
It is very significant that Luke’s genealogy of Jesus is one of the most contested portions of scripture, causing many sceptics to raise questions about the authentic identity of Jesus. If you were compare Luke’s genealogy with Matthew’s they are very different. Luke includes many names that Matthew doesn’t. Between Jesus and David, Matthew records 28 generations, as Luke Records 43 because his genealogy is more comprehensive.

Matthew left out a number of names because his purpose for recording the genealogy was very different to Luke’s. The problem is that between Jesus and David, Luke's list has very different names to Matthew’s. In Matthew, Joseph’s father is called Jacob, while in Luke he is called Heli. After David is mentioned, the list are the same, then Luke goes past Abraham to Adam.

There have been many attempts over the years to try and reconcile these two genealogies. Some of these theories are very complicated and convoluted. One that is popular amongst evangelical scholars is what is known as the Metoridlineal Theory, which suggests that Luke traces Jesus’ genealogy through his mother Mary and not Joseph his step father. On the surface this sound very plausible, however, there are a number of problems with it. This is a recently new theory, it was first suggested in 1490 by Annius of Viterbo who was an Italian Dominican friar, scholar, and historian. I personally don’t agree with this theory because, Luke was a historian and he was very careful about what he recorded, and he tells us that Heli is the father of Joseph.

One possible solution could be that Matthew traces his line through Joseph’s mother and Luke traces it through his father. So the Jacob of Matthew 1:16 may be Joseph’s grandfather on his mothers side. And the Heli of Luke 3:23 may be Joseph’s actual father. We don’t really know.

So what can we learn from a list of 77 names?

1. The significance of the unknown is an essential part of Jesus’ story. As you read through this genealogy you will discover 36 names we know nothing about, some are not even mentioned in the Old Testament. People with no profile, just ordinary people who had an essential part in Jesus’ story.

The story of Jesus and His kingdom, is really a story about anonymous people who choose to live their lives for God as best as they could.

Let me illustrate:-

Tobias and Ollie Ham owned a farm in Allen County near Scottsville, Kentucky. Tobias [1847-1935] was a part time farmer and pastor, living in poverty. You probably have never heard of him. He had a son called Mordecai who became an evangelist. Mordecai was a controversial but successful evangelist. Now there was a farmworker called Albert McMakin, who you have probably never heard of either, invited his employers’ 16 year old son to a mission held by Mordecai in Charlotte in 1934. The son was called Billy, Billy Graham.

The genealogy of Jesus teaches that for the most part, God fulfils his purposes in history through the lives of anonymous people whose lives appear to be unremarkable, but they are an essential part of Jesus’ story.

Feelings of anonymity can cause a person to feel unnoticed, unimportant, of no value with nothing to contribute.

Today, there is a craving for notoriety, people want their 15 minutes of fame. The celebrity pop culture dominates social media, everybody wants to be a somebody. There is something known as FOBI which is the fear of being insignificant, and yet if you were to look at Luke’s genealogy of Jesus there seems to be more unknown than known.

Eli. Nothing else is known of him.

Matthat. Nothing else is known of him.

Levi. Nothing else is known of him.

Jannai. Nothing else is known of him.

Joseph. Nothing else is known of this Joseph.

Mattathias Nothing else is known of him.

Amos. Nothing else is known of him.

Nahum. Nothing else is known of him.

Hesil. Nothing else is known of him.

Naggai. Nothing else is known of him.

There are ten other names in Luke’s genealogy that we know absolutely nothing about.

We need to try and grasp the fact that being a part of Jesus’ story is the most significant thing about us. We may not be part of his genealogy, but we are part of his story. We may not be well known, or greatly gifted, but we are part of his story. Our achievements may be modest, our ministry may be limited but we are part of his story.
prayer: Father thank you that being anonymous does not rob us of significance.
html:
Finding your place in the story of Jesus. Part 2

Study Text: Luke 3:23-38
‘’Now Jesus himself was about thirty years old when he began his ministry. He was the son, so it was thought, of Joseph, the son of Heli, the son of Matthat, the son of Levi, the son of Melki, the son of Jannai, the son of Joseph, the son of Mattathias, the son of Amos, the son of Nahum, the son of Esli, the son of Naggai, the son of Maath, the son of Mattathias, the son of Semein, the son of Josek, the son of Joda, the son of Joanan, the son of Rhesa, the son of Zerubbabel, the son of Shealtiel, the son of Neri the son of Melki, the son of Addi, the son of Cosam, the son of Elmadam, the son of Er, the son of Joshua, the son of Eliezer, the son of Jorim, the son of Matthat, he son of Levi, the son of Simeon the son of Judah, the son of Joseph, the son of Jonam, the son of Eliakim, the son of Melea, the son of Menna, the son of Mattatha, the son of Nathan, the son of David, the son of Jesse, the son of Obed, the son of Boaz, the son of Salmon, the son of Nahshon, the son of Amminadab, the son of Ram the son of Hezron, the son of Perez, the son of Judah, the son of Jacob, the son of Isaac, the son of Abraham, the son of Terah, the son of Nahor, the son of Serug, the son of Reu, the son of Peleg, the son of Eber, the son of Shelah, the son of Cainan, the son of Arphaxad, the son of Shem, the son of Noah, the son of Lamech, the son of Methuselah, the son of Enoch, the son of Jared, the son of Mahalalel, the son of Kenan, the son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God.’’

It is very significant that Luke’s genealogy of Jesus is one of the most contested portions of scripture, causing many sceptics to raise questions about the authentic identity of Jesus. If you were compare Luke’s genealogy with Matthew’s they are very different. Luke includes many names that Matthew doesn’t. After David is mentioned, the list are the same, then Luke goes past Abraham to Adam.

One that is popular amongst evangelical scholars is what is known as the Metoridlineal Theory, which suggests that Luke traces Jesus’ genealogy through his mother Mary and not Joseph his step father. On the surface this sound very plausible, however, there are a number of problems with it. This is a recently new theory, it was first suggested in 1490 by Annius of Viterbo who was an Italian Dominican friar, scholar, and historian. One possible solution could be that Matthew traces his line through Joseph’s mother and Luke traces it through his father. So the Jacob of Matthew 1:16 may be Joseph’s grandfather on his mothers side. And the Heli of Luke 3:23 may be Joseph’s actual father. We don’t really know.

1. The significance of the unknown is an essential part of Jesus’ story. As you read through this genealogy you will discover 36 names we know nothing about, some are not even mentioned in the Old Testament. People with no profile, just ordinary people who had an essential part in Jesus’ story.

The story of Jesus and His kingdom, is really a story about anonymous people who choose to live their lives for God as best as they could.

Let me illustrate:-

Tobias and Ollie Ham owned a farm in Allen County near Scottsville, Kentucky. Tobias [1847-1935] was a part time farmer and pastor, living in poverty. You probably have never heard of him. He had a son called Mordecai who became an evangelist. Mordecai was a controversial but successful evangelist. Now there was a farmworker called Albert McMakin, who you have probably never heard of either, invited his employers’ 16 year old son to a mission held by Mordecai in Charlotte in 1934. The son was called Billy, Billy Graham.

The genealogy of Jesus teaches that for the most part, God fulfils his purposes in history through the lives of anonymous people whose lives appear to be unremarkable, but they are an essential part of Jesus’ story.

Feelings of anonymity can cause a person to feel unnoticed, unimportant, of no value with nothing to contribute.

Today, there is a craving for notoriety, people want their 15 minutes of fame. The celebrity pop culture dominates social media, everybody wants to be a somebody. There is something known as FOBI which is the fear of being insignificant, and yet if you were to look at Luke’s genealogy of Jesus there seems to be more unknown than known.

Eli. Nothing else is known of him.

Matthat. Nothing else is known of him.

Levi. Nothing else is known of him.

Jannai. Nothing else is known of him.

Joseph. Nothing else is known of this Joseph.

Mattathias Nothing else is known of him.

Amos. Nothing else is known of him.

Nahum. Nothing else is known of him.

Hesil. Nothing else is known of him.

Naggai. Nothing else is known of him.

There are ten other names in Luke’s genealogy that we know absolutely nothing about.

We need to try and grasp the fact that being a part of Jesus’ story is the most significant thing about us. We may not be part of his genealogy, but we are part of his story. We may not be well known, or greatly gifted, but we are part of his story. Our achievements may be modest, our ministry may be limited but we are part of his story.

Prayer: Father thank you that being anonymous does not rob us of significance.
